title: Design an E-Commerce Cart API
problemStatement: Design a RESTful API for an e-commerce shopping cart. Users can browse products, add items to cart, update quantities, remove items, and checkout. The cart should handle inventory checks and calculate totals.
functionalRequirements: List products with search, category filter, and pagination
Add item to cart with quantity
constraints: Cannot add more items than available inventory
Cart is tied to the authenticated user
Checkout should validate inventory before processing
Price is calculated server-side (never trust client prices)

rubric.reasoning: Seven endpoints span two domains: products (public catalog) and cart (user-specific state). Separating /products from /cart makes it clear which require auth and which are public.

criteria: POST for adding, PUT for updating, DELETE for removing
rubric.reasoning: POST /cart/items creates a new cart entry. PUT updates quantity (idempotent — setting quantity=3 twice gives the same result). DELETE removes. POST /checkout creates a new order resource.

409 Conflict is key for inventory issues — another user bought the last item while you had it in your cart. This is fundamentally different from 400 (your request is malformed) or 422 (your data fails validation).

E-commerce has more error states than simple CRUD. 'Out of stock' can happen between adding to cart and checkout — the API must re-validate at checkout time. 'Empty cart checkout' prevents wasted payment processing.
Products are public (no auth to browse). Cart is user-specific — without auth, whose cart are we modifying? Guest carts can use session tokens but add complexity.
Product catalogs can have thousands of items. Pagination is essential. Consider cursor-based for infinite scroll UIs, offset for traditional page navigation.
Product search (?search=laptop), category filter (?category=electronics), and sorting (?sort=price,-rating) are essential for usable product browsing.

criteria: Cart totals, line items with prices
rubric.reasoning: Cart responses must include server-calculated totals. Never trust client-side price calculations — a malicious client could send total=0. Include per-item prices so clients can show line items.

defendQuestions.question: Two users add the last item to their carts simultaneously. How does your API prevent overselling?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: This is a classic race condition. Solutions: (1) Optimistic: don't reserve inventory on add-to-cart, check at checkout with a database transaction (SELECT FOR UPDATE). If inventory is 0, return 409. (2) Pessimistic: reserve inventory on add-to-cart with a timeout (e.g., 15 min hold), release if not checked out. Most e-commerce sites use optimistic — it's simpler and avoids holding inventory for abandoned carts.

defendQuestions.question: Why do you calculate prices server-side instead of accepting the price from the client? What if the price changes after adding to cart?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Never trust client prices — a malicious client could send price=0.01 for a $500 item. Always calculate server-side using the product's current price. If the price changes between add-to-cart and checkout, you have two options: (1) always use the latest price (Amazon's approach), or (2) honor the cart price for a limited time. Either way, show the final total at checkout for transparency.

defendQuestions.question: Should the cart persist after logout? What about guest users who haven't created an account?
defendQuestions.modelReasoning: Persisted carts reduce abandoned cart rates — users expect their cart to survive across sessions. For guests, use a session cookie or localStorage with a temporary cart ID. On registration, merge the guest cart with the new user's cart. Conflict resolution: if a guest adds Item A and then logs into an account that already has Item A in the cart, increase quantity or keep the max. This is a UX decision, not just an API decision.

endpoints.description: List products with search, filter, and pagination
Get product details including inventory count
Get current user's cart with calculated totals
Add an item to the cart
Remove an item from the cart
Process checkout — validate inventory and create order

designNotes: Prices should always be calculated server-side — never accept price from the client
Use 409 Conflict for inventory issues (another user bought the last item)
Cart totals should include subtotal, tax, and total for transparency
Checkout should re-validate inventory atomically to prevent race conditions
POST /cart/checkout (not PUT) because it creates a new order resource
